questions.question: Explain the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation. Give one example of where each occurs in memory management.
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted memory within allocated partition
External fragmentation: scattered free memory that cannot be combined
Example: Internal fragmentation occurs in fixed partition allocation
Example: External fragmentation occurs in variable partition allocation

questions.question: Describe the four common strategies for allocating variable-sized partitions: First Fit, Best Fit, Worst Fit, and Next Fit. Compare any two of them.
keyPoints: First Fit: allocate first hole large enough, fast
Best Fit: allocate smallest hole large enough, minimizes leftover space
Worst Fit: allocate largest hole, leaves large remaining pieces
Next Fit: like First Fit but continues from last allocation point

questions.question: What is memory compaction? Explain why it is necessary and mention its disadvantages.
keyPoints: Compaction moves all free memory together to form one large block
Necessary to combat external fragmentation
Requires relocation of all processes
Disadvantage: consumes significant CPU time
Disadvantage: requires processes to be movable (relocation capability)

questions.question: How do base and limit registers provide memory protection in contiguous memory allocation?
keyPoints: Base register stores starting address of process
Limit register stores size of process memory region
CPU adds base to logical address to get physical address
CPU checks if logical address is less than limit
Any violation generates a trap/interrupt

questions.question: Explain what is meant by relocation in memory management. Why is it necessary in contiguous memory allocation?
keyPoints: Relocation: adjusting memory addresses when process loads at different location
Necessary because process can be placed anywhere in memory
All addresses in program must be adjusted by adding base address
Allows multiple processes to be loaded at different addresses
Enables swapping and compaction

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of contiguous memory allocation?
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple to implement, fast memory access (locality), supports direct access
Disadvantages: Suffers from external fragmentation, limited by largest contiguous block
Disadvantages: Internal fragmentation in fixed partitions, requires compaction

questions.question: Describe how a bitmap can be used to track free memory in contiguous allocation. What are its advantages?
keyPoints: Memory divided into allocation units (blocks)
Bitmap: one bit per unit, 1=allocated, 0=free
Finding free space: search for consecutive 0 bits
Advantages: simple data structure, efficient space
Disadvantages: fixed unit size may not match allocation needs

questions.question: Consider a memory with initial hole sizes: 100KB, 300KB, 200KB, 150KB, and 250KB (in that order). Processes arrive requesting: P1: 100KB, P2: 180KB, P3: 50KB. Using First Fit, Best Fit, and Worst Fit strategies, show how memory is allocated. Calculate any fragmentation that occurs.
keyPoints: First Fit: P1 goes to first hole (100KB fits exactly), remaining holes: 0,300,200,150,250
P2 (180KB): goes to 300KB hole, leaves 120KB hole, remaining: 0,120,200,150,250
P3 (50KB): goes to first hole (120KB), leaves 70KB
Best Fit: P1 to 100KB hole (exact), P2 to 150KB hole (smallest fit), P3 to 70KB hole after P2
Worst Fit: P1 to 300KB hole, P2 to 250KB hole, P3 to 200KB hole
Calculate internal/external fragmentation for each

questions.question: Explain the difference between fixed partition allocation and variable partition allocation. Discuss the types of fragmentation that occur in each and suggest scenarios where each would be appropriate.
keyPoints: Fixed partition: memory divided into predetermined partitions at system start
Variable partition: partitions created dynamically based on process sizes
Fixed: suffers mainly from internal fragmentation
Variable: suffers from external fragmentation (reduced internal fragmentation)
Fixed suitable for: known number of processes, simple systems
Variable suitable for: varied process sizes, more efficient memory use
Comparison of advantages/disadvantages of each approach

questions.question: What is external fragmentation? Explain any two techniques used to control or eliminate external fragmentation in contiguous memory allocation systems.
keyPoints: External fragmentation: total free memory sufficient but not contiguous
Technique 1: Compaction - move all processes to consolidate free space
Technique 2: Paging - allows non-contiguous allocation (though not pure contiguous)
Technique 3: Careful allocation strategy selection (Best Fit, etc.)
Discuss pros and cons of each technique
Compaction: CPU intensive, requires relocation capability
Paging: adds complexity, page table overhead
